_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against NICE CKS Hidradenitis Suppurativa, EHSF International Consensus, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Hidradenitis Suppurativa: Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS), also known as acne inversa, is a chronic, inflammatory, recurrent skin disease affecting the intertriginous areas (armpits, groin, under breasts, perianal region) where apocrine glands are concentrated. The condition develops when hair follicles in these areas become obstructed, leading to rupture, inflammation, and sinus tract formation that causes painful nodules, abscesses, and scarring. HS affects approximately 1-4% of the global population, predominantly women and people assigned female at birth, with onset typically in late adolescence to early adulthood. The disease follows a chronic, progressive course and causes severe pain, malodorous discharge, and significant psychosocial burden. While not curable, modern treatments including biologics (TNF-alpha inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors), hormonal therapies, and surgical approaches can effectively control disease and induce remission in many patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1-4% of the global population; often underdiagnosed and misdiagnosed for years before diagnosis
keyStats.demographics: Women and people assigned female at birth are 3 times more likely to be affected; onset typically in late teens to early 20s; rare before puberty and after menopause
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset is 19-22 years; average delay from symptom onset to diagnosis is 7-10 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 74-140 million people worldwide; prevalence similar across all ethnicities

definition: Hidradenitis suppurativa (ICD-10: L73.2) is a chronic, inflammatory, follicular skin disease with a characteristic distribution in apocrine gland-bearing areas. The pathophysiology involves follicular occlusion (hyperkeratosis and plugging of the infundibular portion of the hair follicle), followed by dilatation, rupture, and release of follicular contents (keratin, bacteria, lipids) into the dermis, triggering a neutrophilic inflammatory response. This creates painful nodules and abscesses that either heal spontaneously or progress to form chronic sinus tracts and scarring. The disease runs a chronic, progressive course with flares and remissions. HS is strongly associated with metabolic syndrome, obesity, smoking, and other inflammatory conditions (Crohn's disease, arthritis, depression). Management requires a multidisciplinary team: dermatologists, surgeons, pain specialists, and mental health professionals.

typesClassification.description: Single or multiple localized abscesses without sinus tract formation. Recurrent abscesses may occur but there is no scarring or sinus tract formation. Intermittent flares with relatively asymptomatic periods between episodes.
Recurrent abscesses with sinus tract formation and scarring. Multiple recurrent lesions in one or more areas. Tunnels (sinus tracts) connect abscesses under the skin. Scarring is present but limited.

Diffuse involvement of an anatomic area with multiple interconnected sinus tracts and extensive scarring. Chronic, persistent disease with frequent flares. Abscesses are recurrent and widespread throughout the affected region. Significant tissue destruction and deformity may occur.

typesClassification.type: Follicular Occlusion Variant — Dissecting Cellulitis of the Scalp
typesClassification.description: A related follicular occlusion condition affecting the scalp, presenting with nodules, abscesses, and sinus tracts on the scalp. May co-occur with HS in the same patient. Managed similarly to HS.
A related follicular occlusion condition in the sacrococcygeal region. May co-occur with HS and shares similar pathophysiology and treatment approaches.

primarySymptoms: Painful nodules and abscesses — the hallmark presentation. Deep-seated, tender, firm nodules typically 0.5-3cm in diameter appear in intertriginous areas. The nodules are often very painful and may be preceded by a sensation of pressure. Rupture produces a purulent, often malodorous discharge.

Recurrent flares — HS is characterized by episodic flares with painful, draining lesions followed by periods of relative quiescence. Flares are triggered by friction, heat, sweating, hormonal changes, and stress.

Sinus tract formation — chronic, unruptured abscesses progress to form subcutaneous tunnels (sinus tracts) connecting separate lesions under the skin. These tracts do not heal spontaneously and are a hallmark of moderate-to-severe disease.
Scar formation — repeated inflammation and healing produces scarring: rope-likebridging scars, atrophic scars, and contractures that can restrict movement (particularly in the axillae and groin).
Double-headed comedones — the pathognomonic early sign. Follicular occlusion produces paired, closely adjacent comedones ('tombstone comedones') that precede nodule formation.
Malodorous discharge — discharge from ruptured abscesses has a characteristic foul odor due to bacterial overgrowth, causing significant social distress.
Draining tunnels — established sinus tracts periodically drain through one or more skin openings, often with minimal associated inflammation.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent painful lumps in armpits, groin, under breasts, or perianal area — particularly if they rupture and drain
Double-ended comedones (two closely adjacent blackheads) in intertriginous areas — the pathognomonic early sign of HS
Painful nodules that persist for weeks, rupture with purulent discharge, then may resolve — followed by recurrence at the same or nearby sites
Scarring in intertriginous areas with no clear history of injury — rope-like or atrophic scars indicate chronic disease
Symptoms worsening with heat, sweating, friction, or tight clothing — intertriginous areas are particularly affected
emergencySigns: Extensive cellulitis with fever, severe pain, and systemic symptoms — secondary bacterial infection can complicate HS; urgent medical evaluation and possible IV antibiotics required
Significant sepsis or systemic infection — in severe, neglected cases, systemic infection can develop; requires urgent hospitalization
Rapidly progressive disease with extensive sinus tract involvement — severe HS with extensive involvement may require urgent surgical evaluation
Severe pain unresponsive to usual management — severe pain requires reassessment and optimization of pain management
Significant psychological distress with suicidal ideation — the psychosocial burden of HS is severe; patients with depression or suicidal thoughts require urgent mental health support

causes.description: The primary initiating event. Abnormal follicular hyperkeratosis (thickening of the follicular epithelium) and plugging of the infundibulum causes follicular dilatation. Rupture releases follicular contents (keratin, bacteria, lipids, inflammatory mediators) into the dermis, triggering a robust neutrophilic inflammatory response. This mechanism is shared with acne, dissecting cellulitis of the scalp, and pilonidal disease — collectively called 'follicular occlusion tetrad.'

causes.cause: Dysregulation of the innate immune system
causes.description: HS is driven by a dysregulated innate immune response. The follicular rupture triggers release of IL-1 beta, IL-17, IL-23, TNF-alpha, and other pro-inflammatory cytokines. The IL-23/IL-17 axis and TNF-alpha are central to disease pathogenesis. Neutrophilic infiltrate predominates in early lesions; macrophages and lymphocytes predominate in chronic disease.

HS lesions harbor a polymicrobial community including Staphylococcus species (particularly coagulase-negative staphylococci), anaerobic bacteria (Prevotella, Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus), and Cutibacterium acnes. The microbiome is altered compared to normal skin. Bacterial biofilms form in sinus tracts, contributing to chronicity and antibiotic resistance.

HS shows familial aggregation — approximately 30-40% of patients have a first-degree relative with HS. Autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance is suspected. Mutations in the gamma-secretase gene (NCSTN, PSEN1, PSENEN) are identified in a minority of familial cases. Sporadic cases also have genetic contribution — GWAS studies have identified several associated loci.

Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for HS — 70-90% of HS patients are current or former smokers. Smoking contributes to disease through multiple mechanisms: follicular epithelial thickening, impaired neutrophil function, increased pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and induction of oxidative stress. Smoking cessation is the most important lifestyle intervention for HS patients.

Obesity is strongly associated with HS — approximately 60-80% of HS patients are overweight or obese. Obesity contributes through mechanical friction in intertriginous areas, insulin resistance (promoting inflammation), and adipokine dysregulation. Weight loss improves HS in many patients.

riskFactors.description: Women and people assigned female at birth are 2-3 times more likely to have HS. The higher prevalence is related to differences in apocrine gland distribution and hormonal influences. Men who get HS tend to have more severe disease (particularly perianal involvement).

HS onset is almost always after puberty and before age 40, with peak onset in late teens to early 20s. The disease typically follows a chronic course after onset. Very rare before puberty and after natural menopause.

Approximately 30-40% of HS patients have a first-degree relative with the condition. Familial HS tends to have earlier onset and more severe disease. Mutations in gamma-secretase genes are found in familial cases.

Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor — 70-90% of HS patients are smokers. Smoking cessation is the single most impactful lifestyle change for HS patients. Risk increases with duration and intensity of smoking.

BMI is strongly correlated with HS prevalence and severity. Obesity increases mechanical friction in intertriginous areas, promotes insulin resistance and systemic inflammation, and worsens disease. Weight loss improves outcomes in many patients.

Mechanical stress on intertriginous skin (tight clothing, shaving, friction from skin-on-skin contact) can precipitate flares. Patients should avoid tight clothing, use absorbent powders, and minimize shaving of affected areas.

Heat and humidity worsen HS by increasing sweating and maceration in intertriginous areas. Flares are more common in hot weather, after exercise, and in warm climates. Keeping affected areas cool and dry reduces flare frequency.

affectedDemographics: HS affects approximately 1-4% of the global population — an estimated 74-140 million people worldwide
Women and people assigned female at birth are 2-3 times more likely to be diagnosed; however, perianal HS is more common in men
Onset is almost always after puberty (rare before age 11) and typically in late teens to early 20s; average age at diagnosis is 26-30 years
Average delay from symptom onset to diagnosis is 7-10 years — patients often see multiple physicians before diagnosis is made

HS is associated with significant comorbidities: obesity (60-80%), smoking (70-90%), metabolic syndrome (30-50%), inflammatory bowel disease (especially Crohn's disease, 5-10%), arthritis (10-15%), and depression (15-30%)

The disease causes severe quality of life impairment — HS ranks among the highest of all dermatologic conditions for quality of life impact, comparable to severe psoriasis and inflammatory bowel disease
diagnosisOverview: HS diagnosis is clinical, based on history and characteristic physical findings. The diagnostic triad requires: (1) typical lesions (deep-seated painful nodules, abscesses, sinus tracts, double-ended comedones) in typical locations (axillae, inframammary folds, groin, perineum, perianal region, buttocks); (2) chronic, recurrent course; (3) onset after puberty. The Hurley staging system (I-III) classifies severity. History should cover: disease duration, flare frequency, sites affected, current treatments, smoking history, BMI, associated symptoms (pain, drainage, fever), and comorbidities (IBD, arthritis, depression). Differential diagnosis includes: furuncles/cellulitis, Crohn's perianal disease, Bartholin gland cyst, lymphadenopathy, and pilonidal disease. A skin biopsy is not routinely required but can exclude other conditions in atypical presentations.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The cornerstone of diagnosis. Characteristic lesions (nodules, abscesses, sinus tracts, double-ended comedones) in typical intertriginous locations with chronic, recurrent course establishes the diagnosis. Hurley staging classifies severity. No laboratory test is confirmatory.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination of affected areas (axillae, groin, inframammary, perianal). A clinician will assess for nodules, abscesses, draining sinus tracts, and scarring. Photographs may be taken to track disease over time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A validated measure of treatment response, tracking the number of abscesses and inflammatory nodules (AN count) alongside absence of sinus tract drainage. The primary endpoint in most HS clinical trials.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinical assessment of lesion counts and drainage status at each visit. Performed at baseline and follow-up visits to track response to treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In cases of suspected secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis, fever), a culture of purulent discharge identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities. Bacterial infection is common in severe, draining HS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab of discharge from an abscess or draining sinus tract. Results in 2-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated in moderate-to-severe HS and can be used to track disease activity and treatment response. Elevated CRP is also a cardiovascular risk marker in this population.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Patients with HS should be screened for associated comorbidities including metabolic syndrome (fasting glucose, lipids), inflammatory bowel disease (if GI symptoms), arthritis (if joint symptoms), and depression/anxiety.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Targeted screening based on symptoms: fasting glucose and lipid panel, referral to gastroenterology or rheumatology as indicated, PHQ-9 for depression screening.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for HS follows a staged approach based on Hurley stage and impact on quality of life. For all patients: smoking cessation (critical), weight loss (if overweight), gentle hygiene with antimicrobial washes (chlorhexidine), and avoidance of tight clothing and shaving. For Hurley I-II: topical clindamycin (anti-inflammatory, antibacterial), intralesional corticosteroids (for individual flares), short courses of oral antibiotics (doxycycline, clindamycin + rifampin combination), hormonal therapies (combined oral contraceptives, spironolactone for women), and retinoids (acitretin, isotretinoin for select cases). For Hurley II-III and treatment-resistant disease: systemic biologics are first-line — adalimumab (FDA-approved for HS, anti-TNF), infliximab (anti-TNF), and secukinumab or ixekizumab (anti-IL-17). Other systemic immunosuppressants (methotrexate, cyclosporine) may be used. Surgical management includes: incision and drainage (for acute abscesses), deroofing of sinus tracts, local excision, and radical excision for severe disease. Pain management and psychological support are essential components of care.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical clindamycin 1%
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for mild HS. Clindamycin has anti-inflammatory effects (inhibits T cell activation) and antibacterial activity against Cutibacterium and Staphylococcus. Applied twice daily to affected areas. Inexpensive and well-tolerated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest efficacy for mild Hurley I disease — reduces lesions by approximately 30-50% vs placebo. Less effective for moderate-to-severe disease. Useful as adjunct to systemic therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics — Doxycycline 100mg BID or Clindamycin 300mg BID + Rifampin 300mg BID (combination therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral antibiotics are a cornerstone of HS management, particularly for moderate disease. Doxycycline provides anti-inflammatory and antibacterial effects. Clindamycin-rifampin combination is particularly effective for sinus tracts and moderate disease (4-month courses are standard).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Doxycycline: 30-50% reduction in lesion count. Clindamycin-rifampin: 50-70% reduction in Hurley II disease. Response typically at 4-8 weeks. Long-term use may select for resistance.

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab (Humira) — 160mg week 0, 80mg week 2, then 40mg weekly
medicalTreatments.description: A TNF-alpha inhibitor, FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe HS. Adalimumab is the first and most established biologic for HS. Administered by subcutaneous injection. Reduces inflammatory cytokine production and neutrophil recruitment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves HiSCR (clinical response) in 40-60% of patients after 12-16 weeks. 20-30% achieve clear or almost clear skin. Response is maintained long-term in most responders. Requires monitoring for infections and malignancy.

medicalTreatments.name: Infliximab (Remicade) — 5-10mg/kg infusions every 8 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: An anti-TNF monoclonal antibody used off-label for moderate-to-severe HS not responding to adalimumab. Particularly effective for severe, inflammatory HS with extensive sinus tracts. Administered as IV infusion in an infusion center.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Higher efficacy than adalimumab in some head-to-head data — 60-70% achieve HiSCR. Rapid onset of action. Requires IV access and infusion monitoring.

medicalTreatments.name: Secukinumab or Ixekizumab (IL-17 inhibitors)
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibodies targeting IL-17A (secukinumab) or both IL-17A and IL-17F (ixekizumab). These cytokines are central to HS pathophysiology. Used off-label for HS not responding to anti-TNF therapy or as first biologic in some settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Emerging evidence shows 40-60% HiSCR rates in refractory HS. Particularly effective for patients with concurrent psoriasis or psoriatic arthritis.

medicalTreatments.name: Hormonal therapies for women — Spironolactone 50-200mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: An anti-androgen that reduces sebaceous and apocrine gland activity. Particularly useful for women with menstrual worsening of HS. Takes 3-6 months for full effect. Teratogenic — requires contraception.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 50-70% of women with HS. Reduces lesion frequency and severity. Well-tolerated; side effects include menstrual irregularity, breast tenderness, and hyperkalemia (monitor potassium).

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide 10-40mg/ml
medicalTreatments.description: For individual, painful nodules or abscesses, intralesional corticosteroid injection reduces inflammation, pain, and may prevent progression to rupture. Useful for acute flares.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid symptom relief (within days) for injected lesions. Does not prevent new lesions. Can be combined with systemic therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage (I&D)
surgicalOptions.description: For large, painful, acute abscesses that have not ruptured. Provides rapid pain relief and drainage. However, recurrence at the same site is common, and I&D alone does not address underlying sinus tracts.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate relief but high recurrence rate at the same site (50-80%). Not a definitive treatment for chronic HS. Appropriate for acute abscess management but not for ongoing disease control.

surgicalOptions.name: Deroofing of sinus tracts
surgicalOptions.description: A conservative surgical technique where the roof of sinus tracts is removed (unroofed), leaving the base to heal by secondary intention. Preserves surrounding tissue while eliminating the sinus tract. Can be performed in clinic under local anesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good long-term control of individual sinus tracts with 60-80% lesion resolution. Lower recurrence than I&D alone. Minimal scarring. Can be repeated for recurrent areas.

surgicalOptions.name: Wide local excision
surgicalOptions.description: Complete surgical removal of affected skin in a localized area (e.g., one axilla, one groin). For recurrent, refractory disease in a single anatomical area. Can be performed with primary closure, healing by secondary intention, or skin grafting.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive cure for the excised area (recurrence in that area is essentially 0%). However, HS can appear in other areas. Healing can be prolonged (weeks to months). May require skin grafting for large defects.

surgicalOptions.name: Radical excision (perianal/perineal HS)
surgicalOptions.description: Extensive excision of all affected tissue in the perianal and perineal region for severe, refractory perianal HS. This is a major surgical procedure requiring specialized colorectal surgical expertise.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive for the excised area but significant morbidity and healing time. Perianal HS is challenging to manage surgically due to proximity to sphincter muscles.

surgicalOptions.name: CO2 laser ablation
surgicalOptions.description: Ablative CO2 laser vaporizes affected tissue and sinus tracts, treating multiple areas simultaneously. Can be used for moderate-to-severe HS as an alternative to radical excision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective for sinus tract ablation with good healing and lower morbidity than radical excision. May require multiple sessions. 60-70% improvement in disease severity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation
alternativeTreatments.description: The single most important lifestyle intervention for HS. Smoking contributes to follicular plugging, immune dysregulation, and inflammation. Smoking cessation significantly improves disease outcomes. Support with nicotine replacement, varenicline, or bupropion.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Disease improvement is significant and progressive after cessation. All HS patients who smoke should be offered cessation support. Not optional — it is essential.

alternativeTreatments.name: Weight loss
alternativeTreatments.description: For patients with BMI >25, weight loss improves HS through reduced friction in intertriginous areas, improved insulin sensitivity, and decreased systemic inflammation. Even modest weight loss (5-10% of body weight) can meaningfully reduce disease activity.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Weight loss of 5-10% of body weight is associated with significant reduction in HS severity. Bariatric surgery has been reported to improve or even resolve HS in some patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary modifications
alternativeTreatments.description: Emerging evidence supports dietary modifications including dairy elimination, nightshade avoidance, and low-glycemic/anti-inflammatory diets. Some patients report improvement with these approaches, though evidence quality is limited. Wheat-free and dairy-free diets have the most supporting data.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Individual responses vary. A trial of 4-8 weeks eliminating suspected triggers (dairy, wheat, nightshades, refined sugar) is reasonable. Not all patients respond, but dietary modifications carry minimal risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Stress management and psychological support
alternativeTreatments.description: HS causes severe psychological distress — depression, social anxiety, and impaired quality of life are common. Cognitive behavioral therapy, support groups, and when needed, psychiatric medications are important components of comprehensive care.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Does not treat the skin disease but addresses the psychological burden. Studies show psychological intervention improves quality of life and coping. Essential component of care.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be seen for: any recurrent painful lumps in intertriginous areas (for accurate diagnosis); moderate-to-severe HS requiring prescription therapy; flares not responding to topical treatments; consideration of systemic therapy (biologics, immunosuppressants); surgical management of sinus tracts; and significant psychosocial distress. General practitioners can provide initial diagnosis and prescribe topical therapy but should refer to a dermatologist for moderate-to-severe disease. Co-management with a colorectal surgeon is needed for extensive perianal disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with specific interest in HS — not all dermatologists are comfortable managing moderate-to-severe HS. Ask about their experience with biologics (adalimumab, infliximab, IL-17 inhibitors), surgical techniques (deroofing, excision), and Hurley staging. A dermatologist with access to a multidisciplinary team (colorectal surgery, pain management, mental health) is optimal. For severe, refractory disease, specialized HS clinics (where they exist) offer the most comprehensive care.
preventionStrategies: Smoking cessation — the single most impactful intervention; reduces follicular plugging, inflammation, and disease activity
Weight management — maintaining healthy BMI reduces friction, inflammation, and disease severity
Avoid tight, restrictive clothing — loose, breathable clothing minimizes friction and heat accumulation in intertriginous areas
Avoid shaving intertriginous areas — shaving causes follicular trauma that can precipitate lesions; use depilatory creams or laser hair removal instead if hair removal is desired
Keep intertriginous areas cool and dry — use absorbent powders (zinc oxide, talc-free), wear moisture-wicking fabrics, and avoid prolonged heat exposure
Use antimicrobial washes — chlorhexidine wash in affected areas reduces bacterial colonization and may prevent flares
lifestyleModifications: Wear loose, cotton clothing — minimizes friction and heat in intertriginous areas
Use absorbent powders — zinc oxide or talc-free powders absorb moisture and reduce maceration
Avoid heavy moisturizers in intertriginous areas — thick products trap moisture and worsen maceration
Quit smoking — the most important lifestyle change; available support includes nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion, and behavioral counseling
Exercise in breathable, moisture-wicking clothing — shower and change immediately after exercise to reduce bacterial load in intertriginous areas
Use antibacterial body wash (chlorhexidine 4%) — reduces bacterial colonization in affected areas

recommended: Low-glycemic, anti-inflammatory diet — reduces systemic inflammation and insulin resistance; emphasis on vegetables, legumes, whole grains, lean proteins
Dairy elimination — dairy (particularly whole milk and whey protein) may worsen HS through hormonal and inflammatory mechanisms; a trial of dairy-free diet for 4-8 weeks is reasonable
Omega-3 rich foods — fatty fish (salmon, sardines), walnuts, flaxseed have anti-inflammatory effects
Weight loss foods — if overweight, calorie restriction and reduced processed food intake supports weight loss and reduces HS severity
Nightshade elimination (optional) — some patients report improvement avoiding tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, eggplant; evidence is anecdotal
avoid: Dairy — whole milk, whey protein, and dairy products may worsen HS; dairy-free diet is associated with improvement in some studies
Refined sugars and high-glycemic foods — promote insulin resistance and systemic inflammation; high sugar intake is associated with worse HS
Processed foods and red meat — pro-inflammatory diet may worsen HS
Alcohol in excess — heavy alcohol consumption worsens inflammation; moderate consumption is generally acceptable
Very hot, spicy foods — may increase sweating and maceration in intertriginous areas; individual response varies

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is beneficial and recommended for HS patients, with modifications to minimize intertriginous friction and heat. Wear loose, moisture-wicking fabrics (synthetic athletic wear rather than cotton which holds moisture); shower immediately after exercise to reduce bacterial load and cool skin; use absorbent powders before and after exercise; avoid prolonged exercise in hot, humid conditions; and consider low-friction exercises (swimming, cycling, elliptical) over exercises that involve significant intertriginous contact (long-distance running, rowing). Weight loss through exercise improves HS severity in overweight patients. Swimming is particularly good — the cool water reduces inflammation and buoyancy reduces friction. After swimming, shower and apply powder to intertriginous areas.
dailyManagement: Wash affected areas with chlorhexidine wash 4% daily — reduces bacterial colonization and prevents flares
Apply topical clindamycin as prescribed — twice daily to affected areas for mild disease
Use absorbent powder (zinc oxide) in intertriginous areas — apply after shower and after exercise
Wear loose, breathable clothing — avoid tight underwear, synthetic fabrics, and restrictive garments
Avoid shaving affected areas — use electric clippers or depilatory creams instead if hair removal is needed
Document flares — maintain a symptom diary to identify triggers and track response to treatment
Apply warm compresses to painful nodules — for acute nodules, warm compresses promote rupture and relieve pain
prognosis: HS is a chronic, progressive condition with no cure. Without treatment, disease tends to worsen over time — more lesions, more sinus tracts, more scarring. With modern treatment, disease can be controlled in the majority of patients. Adalimumab achieves HiSCR in 40-60% of patients with moderate-to-severe disease; combination antibiotic therapy controls lesions in 50-70% of patients with moderate disease. Early intervention produces better outcomes — delaying treatment allows progression. Spontaneous remission occurs in a minority of patients, particularly after menopause in women. The disease causes significant, persistent quality of life impairment without treatment. With optimal multidisciplinary care (medical, surgical, psychological), most patients can achieve meaningful disease control.
recoveryTimeline: With topical therapy: improvement at 2-4 weeks, maximal at 8-12 weeks. With oral antibiotics: improvement at 4-8 weeks, maximal at 3-6 months. With adalimumab: initial response at 4-8 weeks, maximal at 12-16 weeks. With infliximab: rapid response within 2-4 weeks. With deroofing: immediate reduction in sinus tract burden; healing takes 4-8 weeks. With wide excision: healing takes 4-12 weeks depending on closure method; definitive for that area. Weight loss: gradual improvement over 3-6 months with sustained weight loss.
complications: Scarring and sinus tract formation — chronic, recurrent inflammation produces rope-like scarring and subcutaneous sinus tracts that do not heal without surgical intervention
Restricted movement — scarring in the axillae and groin can cause contractures that restrict arm and leg movement, particularly after extensive disease
Lymphedema — chronic inflammation and scarring can damage lymphatic drainage, causing lymphedema in the affected limb
Secondary bacterial infection — cellulitis, abscesses, and bacteremia can complicate HS; may require IV antibiotics
Anemia and hypoalbuminemia — chronic suppuration causes protein and blood loss, leading to anemia and low albumin in severe disease
Systemic amyloidosis — rare, but chronic severe HS can lead to AA amyloidosis with renal involvement
Squamous cell carcinoma — a rare but serious complication of long-standing, severe perianal HS (Marjolin's ulcer); requires vigilance and biopsy of any non-healing areas
Severe psychosocial impact — depression, social anxiety, and social isolation are common; quality of life impairment is among the highest of any dermatologic condition

supportResources.name: Hidradenitis Suppurativa Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based nonprofit providing patient education, support groups, physician finder, and research funding specifically for HS.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — HS Patient Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based information from the largest dermatologic professional organization.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS: Hidradenitis Suppurativa. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2023.
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary with evidence-based management recommendations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Furunculosis (Boils)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Single boils or furunculosis presents as isolated abscesses without the chronic, recurrent course, intertriginous distribution, and sinus tract formation characteristic of HS. Boils typically resolve and do not recur in the same pattern.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's Disease (Perianal)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's perianal disease includes fissures, fistulas, and abscesses in the perianal region. Can co-exist with HS. Differentiated by presence of intestinal symptoms, non-caseating granulomas on biopsy, and different fistula patterns.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pilonidal Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pilonidal disease presents with a chronic draining sinus in the sacrococcygeal region. May co-occur with HS. The two are distinguished by location (sacrococcygeal vs intertriginous) and the characteristic sinus tract anatomy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Folliculitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Superficial folliculitis presents with small, superficial pustules at hair follicle openings. HS presents with deep, painful nodules and abscesses in intertriginous areas with chronic course and sinus tract formation. Folliculitis does not form sinus tracts or extensive scarring.

relatedConditions.name: Acne Conglobata
relatedConditions.relevance: Acne conglobata is the most severe form of nodulocystic acne, sharing follicular occlusion pathophysiology with HS. Can co-occur in the same patient. Both respond to isotretinoin and TNF inhibitors.

relatedConditions.name: Dissecting Cellulitis of the Scalp
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of the follicular occlusion tetrad with HS and pilonidal disease. Presents with nodules and sinus tracts on the scalp. May co-occur with HS. Responds to similar treatments.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hidradenitis suppurativa and regular boils?
faqs.answer: Regular boils (furuncles) are isolated infections that typically resolve with treatment and do not recur. HS is chronic, recurrent, and produces a characteristic pattern of painful nodules, abscesses, and sinus tracts in intertriginous areas. The key distinguishing features of HS are: chronic, recurrent course; multiple sites affected; intertriginous distribution (axillae, groin, under breasts, perianal); sinus tract formation; scarring; and double-ended comedones. Single boils in non-intertriginous areas are not HS.

faqs.question: What are the stages of hidradenitis suppurativa?
faqs.answer: HS is classified into three Hurley stages. Stage I (mild): single or multiple abscesses without sinus tract formation. Stage II (moderate): recurrent abscesses with sinus tract formation and scarring. Stage III (severe): diffuse involvement with multiple interconnected sinus tracts and extensive scarring. The stage guides treatment selection — mild disease is managed medically; severe disease often requires surgery in addition to medical therapy.

faqs.question: Is HS genetic?
faqs.answer: HS has a genetic component — approximately 30-40% of patients have a first-degree relative with the condition. Autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance is suspected. Mutations in gamma-secretase genes (NCSTN, PSEN1, PSENEN) are found in a minority of familial cases. However, most cases are sporadic and environmental factors (smoking, obesity) play the major role in disease expression.

faqs.question: What treatments are available for HS?
faqs.answer: Treatment ranges from topical to surgical. Mild HS: topical clindamycin, antimicrobial washes, and intralesional corticosteroids. Moderate HS: oral antibiotics (doxycycline, clindamycin + rifampin), hormonal therapies (spironolactone for women), and retinoids (acitretin). Moderate-to-severe HS: biologic drugs — adalimumab (FDA-approved), infliximab, and IL-17 inhibitors. Surgical: incision and drainage for acute abscesses; deroofing of sinus tracts; wide local excision for severe, refractory disease.

faqs.question: Do biologics work for HS?
faqs.answer: Yes — biologic drugs, particularly adalimumab (anti-TNF) and infliximab (anti-TNF), are the most effective treatments for moderate-to-severe HS. Adalimumab achieves clinical response (HiSCR) in 40-60% of patients; infliximab in 60-70%. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab) are also effective. Biologics reduce inflammation, pain, and sinus tract formation. They require continuous treatment to maintain response.

faqs.question: Can surgery cure HS?
faqs.answer: Surgery can definitively cure HS in the treated area. Wide local excision removes all affected tissue and recurrence in that area is essentially zero. However, HS can appear in other areas that were not treated. Deroofing of sinus tracts (removing the roof while preserving the base) provides good control of individual tracts. Incision and drainage alone is not curative — recurrence at the same site is common. Surgery is typically combined with medical therapy to prevent new lesions in other areas.

faqs.question: Does diet affect HS?
faqs.answer: Diet affects HS for some patients. The most evidence supports dairy elimination (particularly whole milk and whey protein) — some patients improve on a dairy-free diet. A low-glycemic, anti-inflammatory diet may help by reducing insulin resistance and systemic inflammation. Nightshade avoidance (tomatoes, potatoes, peppers) is reported helpful by some patients. Weight loss in overweight patients consistently improves HS. A trial of dietary modifications (4-8 weeks of dairy-free, low-sugar diet) is reasonable for all HS patients.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for HS?
faqs.answer: There is no universally guaranteed cure for HS. However, with modern treatment — biologics, combination antibiotics, surgical management — the majority of patients can achieve meaningful disease control with significantly reduced pain, fewer flares, and improved quality of life. Early treatment prevents progression. Severe, refractory disease can be managed with radical excision. Research into more effective treatments is ongoing.

metaTitle: Hidradenitis Suppurativa: Types, Causes, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS): chronic, painful nodules, abscesses, and sinus tracts in armpits, groin, and under breasts. Learn about stages, biologics (adalimumab), surgery, and lifestyle management.

images.altText: Clinical presentation of hidradenitis suppurativa showing multiple inflammatory nodules and early sinus tract formation in the axillary region
images.caption: Hidradenitis suppurativa typically presents with painful, recurrent nodules and abscesses in intertriginous areas like the armpits.

images.altText: Advanced hidradenitis suppurativa showing extensive sinus tract formation and scarring in the groin and perianal region
images.caption: Advanced HS shows multiple interconnected sinus tracts and rope-like scarring from chronic inflammation.

images.altText: Diagram showing typical distribution of hidradenitis suppurativa affecting intertriginous areas including axillae, groin, and inframammary region
images.caption: HS characteristically affects apocrine gland-bearing intertriginous areas — armpits, groin, under breasts, and perianal region.
